children.name: Senior Government Managers
children.ai_rationale: High-level decision-making involves political judgment, stakeholder management, and accountability structures that are deeply human. AI can assist with policy analysis but not replace executive governance.

children.name: Financial Managers
children.ai_rationale: Financial reporting, forecasting, and compliance tasks are highly automatable. However, strategic capital allocation, investor relations, and organizational leadership remain human domains.

children.name: Human Resources Managers
children.ai_rationale: Recruitment screening, payroll, and policy compliance are increasingly AI-assisted. Employee relations, culture building, and sensitive conflict resolution require human empathy and judgment.

children.name: Retail and Wholesale Trade Managers
children.ai_rationale: Inventory management, scheduling, and sales analytics are increasingly automated. Floor management, staff motivation, and customer relationship roles retain human value.

children.name: Restaurant and Foodservice Managers
children.ai_rationale: Scheduling, ordering, and cost tracking can be automated but the physical, interpersonal, and creative aspects of running a restaurant remain human-dependent.

children.name: Construction Managers
children.ai_rationale: Project scheduling and cost estimation benefit from AI tools, but on-site coordination, safety oversight, and real-time problem solving in physical environments limit full automation.

children.name: Manufacturing Managers
children.ai_rationale: Production optimization and quality control are AI-enhanced, but plant management requires physical presence, worker relations, and rapid real-world problem solving.

children.name: IT Managers
children.ai_rationale: AI dramatically accelerates software delivery and infrastructure management, but technical leadership, architecture decisions, and organizational alignment remain human responsibilities.

children.name: Health Care Managers
children.ai_rationale: Resource allocation and scheduling can be optimized by AI, but managing care quality, staff morale, regulatory compliance, and patient advocacy require human oversight.

children.name: Accountants & Auditors
children.ai_rationale: Bookkeeping, tax preparation, audit sampling, and financial statement generation are highly automatable. AI can already perform much of the routine work with greater speed and accuracy.

children.name: Financial & Investment Analysts
children.ai_rationale: Data analysis, pattern recognition, and report generation are being rapidly automated. However, client relationships, novel market interpretation, and investment committee decisions retain human value.

children.name: Human Resources Specialists
children.ai_rationale: Resume screening, job matching, and compliance tracking are prime targets for AI. Interviewing, culture fit assessment, and sensitive HR conversations require human presence.

children.name: Administrative Officers
children.ai_rationale: Scheduling, document management, correspondence drafting, and data entry are highly automatable. Administrative roles face significant displacement pressure from AI assistants and automation tools.

children.name: Payroll Clerks
children.ai_rationale: Payroll processing is almost entirely rule-based and data-driven, making it one of the highest AI exposure occupations. Modern payroll software already automates the majority of this work.

children.name: Bank Tellers
children.ai_rationale: Digital banking, ATMs, and mobile apps have already decimated this role. AI-powered chatbots and automated teller systems are accelerating further displacement.

children.name: Insurance Underwriters
children.ai_rationale: Risk assessment based on structured data is a core AI strength. Automated underwriting engines are already replacing many routine cases, leaving only complex or novel risks for humans.

children.name: Real Estate Agents
children.ai_rationale: Property valuation and market analysis are AI-assisted, but the trust-based relationship nature of real estate transactions, physical property tours, and negotiation keep humans central.

children.name: Legal Administrative Assistants
children.ai_rationale: Document preparation, scheduling, billing, and legal research assistance are being rapidly transformed by AI legal tools. This role faces high displacement risk.

children.name: Customer Service Representatives
children.ai_rationale: AI chatbots and voice agents are increasingly handling tier-1 support. Complex, emotionally charged, or novel customer issues still require human agents, but the volume is shrinking.

children.name: Software Developers
children.ai_rationale: AI coding assistants dramatically boost productivity but software development involves system design, debugging complex issues, and understanding business requirements — deeply human skills that anchor the role.

children.name: Computer Systems Analysts
children.ai_rationale: Requirements gathering and documentation benefit from AI, but translating organizational needs into technical solutions, managing stakeholders, and designing systems require deep human judgment.

children.name: Data Scientists
children.ai_rationale: Ironically, AI reduces some data science work (automated ML) while creating demand for those who can direct, evaluate, and interpret AI systems. The role is evolving rather than disappearing.

children.name: Cybersecurity Specialists
children.ai_rationale: AI enhances threat detection but attackers also use AI, creating a dynamic arms race requiring constant human expertise. Security strategy, incident response, and policy design remain human domains.

children.name: Civil Engineers
children.ai_rationale: Design and simulation tools are AI-enhanced, but engineering judgment, site-specific problem solving, regulatory approval, and physical infrastructure oversight require human engineers.

children.name: Mechanical Engineers
children.ai_rationale: AI assists with design optimization and simulation, but physical prototyping, manufacturing integration, and multi-system engineering judgment require experienced human engineers.

children.name: Electrical Engineers
children.ai_rationale: Circuit design and simulation benefit from AI tools, but electrical systems integration, safety certification, and novel problem solving in power and electronics remain human-centered.

children.name: Environmental Scientists
children.ai_rationale: Data analysis and modelling are AI-assisted, but field work, regulatory interaction, environmental advocacy, and interpreting novel ecological conditions require human scientists.

children.name: Architects
children.ai_rationale: Generative design tools and BIM software are being transformed by AI, accelerating design iteration. However, client vision translation, contextual judgment, and project stewardship remain human.

children.name: Network Technicians
children.ai_rationale: Network monitoring and diagnostics are increasingly automated, but physical installation, hardware troubleshooting, and on-site infrastructure work require human technicians.

children.name: Geoscientists
children.ai_rationale: AI accelerates seismic data interpretation and resource modelling, but field work, exploration judgment, and regulatory submissions require experienced geoscientists.

children.name: Agricultural Scientists
children.ai_rationale: Crop modelling and genomics benefit from AI, but field experimentation, farmer advisory work, and biological system complexity keep human scientists essential.

children.name: Physicians & Surgeons
children.ai_rationale: AI excels at diagnostic imaging and pattern recognition but clinical judgment, patient relationships, procedural skill, and ethical decision-making in complex cases remain deeply human.

children.name: Registered Nurses
children.ai_rationale: Nursing is fundamentally physical and relational. AI can assist with documentation and monitoring, but hands-on patient care, clinical assessment, and emotional support are irreplaceable human skills.

children.name: Dentists
children.ai_rationale: AI aids in X-ray analysis and treatment planning, but dental procedures require precise manual dexterity, patient interaction, and real-time adaptation that robots cannot currently replicate.

children.name: Pharmacists
children.ai_rationale: Drug dispensing and interaction checking are increasingly automated. However, patient counselling, complex medication management, and clinical pharmacy roles maintain significant human value.

children.name: Physiotherapists
children.ai_rationale: Physical rehabilitation is inherently hands-on and requires continuous assessment of patient movement, pain response, and emotional state. AI can assist with exercise prescription but not treatment delivery.

children.name: Medical Laboratory Technologists
children.ai_rationale: Automated analyzers handle routine tests, and AI is improving pathology image analysis. However, quality control, complex specimen handling, and novel test validation require skilled technologists.

children.name: Radiologists
children.ai_rationale: AI diagnostic imaging tools are among the most mature medical AI applications, with some models matching radiologist accuracy on specific tasks. The role will transform significantly rather than disappear.

children.name: Paramedics
children.ai_rationale: Emergency response requires physical presence, rapid improvisation in chaotic environments, and life-saving hands-on intervention. AI cannot replicate the physical and situational demands of paramedic work.

children.name: Occupational Therapists
children.ai_rationale: OT requires holistic patient assessment, creative problem solving for daily living challenges, and therapeutic relationships that are fundamentally human and physical in nature.

children.name: Dental Hygienists
children.ai_rationale: Oral health assessment and cleaning require manual dexterity, physical presence, and patient interaction skills that are not easily automated.

children.name: Mental Health Counsellors
children.ai_rationale: Therapeutic relationships are built on deep human empathy, trust, and lived experience. AI tools may support but cannot replace the healing power of genuine human connection in mental health care.

children.name: Home Support Workers
children.ai_rationale: Personal care, companionship, and daily living assistance require physical presence and human compassion that robots cannot replicate. This role is driven by demographic demand that will only grow.

children.name: Elementary School Teachers
children.ai_rationale: Teaching children involves socialization, character formation, and responsive human guidance that AI cannot replicate. AI tutoring tools may supplement but not replace teachers, especially at early ages.

children.name: Secondary School Teachers
children.ai_rationale: AI tutoring is more viable at secondary level, but curriculum design, mentorship, classroom management, and preparing students for complex civic life require human teachers.

children.name: University Professors
children.ai_rationale: Research, intellectual discourse, mentorship of graduate students, and original scholarly contribution remain human domains. Routine lecturing is more susceptible to AI-based course delivery.

children.name: Early Childhood Educators
children.ai_rationale: Caring for young children requires constant physical presence, emotional attunement, and human nurturing. This is among the least automatable occupations due to the irreplaceable human bond required.

children.name: Social Workers
children.ai_rationale: Child protection, family intervention, and community advocacy require trust, legal judgment, and navigating complex human systems. AI can support case management but cannot replace human social workers.

children.name: Lawyers
children.ai_rationale: Legal research, contract drafting, and document review are rapidly being transformed by AI. Courtroom advocacy, negotiation, and client counsel retain human judgment, but the ratio is shifting.

children.name: Judges & Magistrates
children.ai_rationale: Judicial decisions require weighing precedent, equity, and social context within a framework of democratic legitimacy. AI may assist with research but judicial authority must remain human.

children.name: Police Officers
children.ai_rationale: Law enforcement requires physical presence, community trust, crisis de-escalation, and real-time judgment in unpredictable situations. AI supports policing but cannot perform it.

children.name: Firefighters
children.ai_rationale: Firefighting involves physical danger, real-time decision making in chaotic environments, and life-saving actions that require human strength, agility, and judgment.

children.name: Psychologists
children.ai_rationale: Psychological assessment and therapy require deep empathy, ethical judgment, and the therapeutic power of human relationship. AI tools may support psychologists but cannot replace clinical practice.

children.name: Librarians
children.ai_rationale: Information retrieval and cataloguing are core AI strengths. Reference librarians face significant pressure as AI search tools become more capable, though community programming and curation roles persist.

children.name: Graphic Designers
children.ai_rationale: AI image generation tools are disrupting entry-level graphic design work significantly. Strategic design thinking, brand identity, and client collaboration retain value, but the field is being transformed.

children.name: Writers & Authors
children.ai_rationale: AI can generate text at scale but original voice, lived experience, and deep creative vision remain human. Journalism, fiction, and personal narrative face disruption at the commodity end.

children.name: Journalists
children.ai_rationale: Routine reporting on structured events (earnings, sports scores) is already being automated. Investigative journalism, source development, and frontline reporting retain irreplaceable human value.

children.name: Musicians & Singers
children.ai_rationale: AI music generation is advancing rapidly, threatening session work and background music. However, live performance, authentic artistic expression, and the human connection in music are resilient.

children.name: Photographers
children.ai_rationale: AI image generation threatens stock photography significantly. However, event photography, portraiture, and editorial work requiring physical presence and creative direction remain human domains.

children.name: Film & Video Editors
children.ai_rationale: AI is accelerating rough cuts and effects work. However, narrative judgment, pacing, and creative storytelling in editing remain distinctly human skills commanding significant value.

children.name: Translators & Interpreters
children.ai_rationale: Machine translation quality has improved dramatically. While literary translation and high-stakes interpretation retain human value, routine document translation faces severe AI disruption.

children.name: Coaches & Instructors
children.ai_rationale: Sports and fitness coaching requires physical demonstration, real-time form assessment, and motivational relationship building that AI cannot replicate in person.

children.name: Retail Salespersons
children.ai_rationale: E-commerce and self-checkout are displacing retail roles. AI product recommendation and inventory management accelerate this. Physical store roles requiring product expertise and service retain some value.

children.name: Food Counter Attendants
children.ai_rationale: Automated kiosks and robotic food preparation are expanding. However, the hospitality element of food service and complex orders in many settings keep humans employed.

children.name: Cooks
children.ai_rationale: Robotic cooking is advancing but remains expensive and inflexible. Human cooks adapt to ingredient variation, customer preferences, and creative menu development in ways machines cannot match.

children.name: Chefs
children.ai_rationale: Culinary creativity, flavor intuition, and kitchen leadership are distinctly human. AI may assist with recipe optimization but cannot replace the artistry and sensory mastery of skilled chefs.

children.name: Hair Stylists & Barbers
children.ai_rationale: Hair cutting and styling requires physical dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and the personal service relationship that is fundamentally human. Automation of this role is not feasible in the foreseeable future.

children.name: Truck Drivers
children.ai_rationale: Autonomous trucking technology is advancing rapidly. Highway long-haul is closer to automation than urban last-mile delivery. This role faces significant medium-term displacement risk.

children.name: Taxi & Rideshare Drivers
children.ai_rationale: Autonomous vehicle technology directly targets this role. Robotaxis are already operating in some cities and will expand significantly, making this one of the most exposed transportation occupations.

children.name: Security Guards
children.ai_rationale: AI surveillance and monitoring systems supplement security guards. However, physical response, access control, and deterrence by human presence keep guards employed despite technology advances.

children.name: Travel Agents
children.ai_rationale: Online booking platforms and AI travel planning tools have already severely disrupted this role. Complex luxury travel and corporate travel management retain some human value.

children.name: Cleaners & Janitors
children.ai_rationale: Robotic vacuums and floor cleaners are emerging but commercial cleaning requires adaptability to varied environments and tasks that make full automation challenging and expensive.

children.name: Child Care Workers
children.ai_rationale: Caring for children requires constant human supervision, emotional attunement, and physical presence. This is one of the least automatable occupations, driven by growing demand.

children.name: Electricians
children.ai_rationale: Electrical work requires physical presence in varied environments, problem solving with hands, and real-time adaptation. The skilled trades shortage makes this role valuable and largely automation-resistant.

children.name: Plumbers
children.ai_rationale: Plumbing requires physical manipulation in tight, varied spaces with bespoke problems. The dexterity and adaptability required makes this highly resistant to automation for the foreseeable future.

children.name: Carpenters
children.ai_rationale: Carpentry requires skilled physical work in diverse settings with constant adaptation. While pre-fab construction is growing, custom carpentry and renovation work remain human domains.

children.name: Welders
children.ai_rationale: Robotic welding is already widespread in automotive manufacturing for repetitive welds. However, custom fabrication, repair work, and complex structural welding retain significant human demand.

children.name: HVAC Technicians
children.ai_rationale: Installation and maintenance of heating and cooling systems requires physical work in diverse environments with real-time problem solving. Smart building systems assist but don't replace technicians.

children.name: Auto Mechanics
children.ai_rationale: Diagnostic software is AI-enhanced but physical repair, adaptation to vehicle variation, and hands-on troubleshooting keep mechanics essential. EV growth is changing skill requirements rather than eliminating the role.

children.name: Heavy Equipment Operators
children.ai_rationale: Autonomous construction and mining equipment is advancing, particularly in mines. However, urban construction with complex environments and safety requirements slows full automation.

children.name: Bus & Transit Drivers
children.ai_rationale: Autonomous transit vehicles are being piloted in multiple cities. Fixed-route, predictable urban transit is more automatable than complex driving tasks, creating medium-term risk.

children.name: Aircraft Pilots
children.ai_rationale: Autopilot handles much of routine flying, but pilot judgment in emergencies, regulatory requirements, and passenger confidence make full automation of commercial aviation a very long-term prospect.

children.name: Bricklayers & Masons
children.ai_rationale: Bricklaying robots exist but handle only standard, accessible work. Complex patterns, repairs, and retrofit work require skilled masons. The trades shortage makes this role valued.

children.name: Farmers & Farm Managers
children.ai_rationale: Precision agriculture AI is transforming farming with drones, sensors, and automated equipment. However, farm management requires biological knowledge, weather adaptation, and physical oversight.

children.name: Agricultural Workers
children.ai_rationale: Robotic harvesting is advancing for some crops. However, many crops require selective picking that robots handle poorly. Labour shortages mean automation is driven by necessity rather than pure economics.

children.name: Fishers
children.ai_rationale: Commercial fishing requires physical work at sea in unpredictable conditions. AI assists with fish finding and route optimization but the physical and environmental nature of fishing limits automation.

children.name: Logging & Forestry Workers
children.ai_rationale: Automated harvesting machinery is well-established in forestry. However, selective harvesting, environmental monitoring, and remote operations require human workers.

children.name: Mining Machine Operators
children.ai_rationale: Autonomous mining vehicles and remote operation are growing rapidly, particularly in underground and open-pit mines. This sector is one of the most active in adopting automation technology.

children.name: Oil & Gas Drillers
children.ai_rationale: Drilling automation and remote monitoring are advancing. However, well control, equipment troubleshooting, and operating in remote conditions require skilled human workers.

children.name: Industrial Butchers
children.ai_rationale: Meat processing automation is advancing but animal anatomy variation makes full automation difficult. Robotic systems handle some cuts while humans manage complex or variable tasks.

children.name: Assembly Line Workers
children.ai_rationale: Repetitive manufacturing assembly is the archetypal automation target. Industrial robots handle the majority of automotive and electronics assembly. This category faces sustained displacement pressure.

children.name: Textile Workers
children.ai_rationale: Textile and garment manufacturing has been heavily automated in developed countries, with remaining workers handling quality control and specialized production. AI inspection tools add further pressure.

children.name: Printing Machine Operators
children.ai_rationale: Digital printing automation and AI-driven prepress workflows have dramatically reduced labour requirements. The print industry continues to contract as digital media displaces physical print.

children.name: Warehouse Workers
children.ai_rationale: Warehouse robotics (picking, sorting, inventory) are rapidly advancing. Amazon-style fulfillment centers demonstrate the potential scale of automation. Last-mile delivery complexity slows full displacement.

children.name: Quality Control Inspectors
children.ai_rationale: AI computer vision is transforming quality inspection, detecting defects with greater accuracy and speed than humans. This role is among the highest-risk manufacturing occupations.

children.name: Machine Operators
children.ai_rationale: CNC and automated manufacturing equipment reduces the need for manual machine operators. AI-driven process optimization further automates parameter adjustment and monitoring.

children.name: Bakers
children.ai_rationale: Industrial baking is heavily automated, but artisan bakeries and specialty products require human skill and sensory judgment. The premium market segment provides resilience for skilled bakers.
